items.q: Was the English Civil War caused by religion alone?
items.correct: No — religion (Laudian reforms, Scottish Presbyterians) combined with financial disputes (ship money), political conflict over parliamentary rights, and Charles I's personal style.
items.misconception: The Civil War was a war of religion between Catholics and Protestants.
items.random_wrong: The Civil War began because Cromwell wanted to be king.
items.why: Both sides were largely Protestant. Reducing the war to religion misses the financial and constitutional disputes; modern historians stress multi-causal explanations.

items.q: What caused the English Civil War?
items.correct: Long-term tensions over royal absolutism, religion (Laudian reforms), and parliamentary rights, combined with Charles I's specific mismanagement and personal rigidity.
items.misconception: It was caused by Charles I trying to arrest five MPs in 1642.
items.random_wrong: It was caused by Henry VIII's break with Rome.
items.why: The arrest attempt was the trigger; the causes were structural. Students mistake the proximate event for the underlying religious-political crisis that had built for decades.

items.q: Why is 1688 called the Glorious Revolution?
items.correct: Parliament invited William of Orange to replace the Catholic James II, with little bloodshed in England, establishing parliamentary supremacy and the 1689 Bill of Rights.
items.misconception: It was glorious because no one died anywhere in the conflict.
items.random_wrong: William of Orange was Charles II's son and inherited the throne.
items.why: It was bloody in Ireland and Scotland (Boyne, Killiecrankie). Students take the English-centric label literally and miss the violent imposition of the new regime elsewhere.

items.q: Which 1605 plot aimed to blow up Parliament?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Gunpowder Plot. “Babington Plot” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What was the 1628 statute curbing Charles I's prerogative known as?
items.why: It's Petition of Right. “Habeas Corpus Act” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did James VI of Scotland become James I of England?
items.why: Exact answer: 1603. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
